The Peanut Gallery was not a gallery.
It was not made of peanuts.
It was, however, occupied by Fornay and Hinton, which meant it was occupied by commentary,
observation, and the particular species of attention that sharpens into wit when left
unsupervised for extended periods.
The space itself defied summary. It was perched—that was the word everyone used, perched—
somewhere between the Chamber’s outer ring and the place where the meadow’s memory
pooled into architecture. A room that was also a balcony. A balcony that was also a nest. Large
windows that might have been windows or might have been absences where windows would
have been redundant, because the view came in anyway, uninvited and welcome.
Through these windows-or-absences, one could see: the gradient in its afternoon
configuration, gold and rose; the meadow’s edge where it met the Chamber’s outer curve; the
path that Bronn and Yuli and Popo had walked earlier, still holding the impression of their
passage; the sky, which was doing something complicated with clouds and light that suggested
either weather or philosophy, possibly both.
Fornay sat in a chair that had the structural integrity of a philosophical argument—which is to
say, it held together through sheer insistence. They had a notebook open on their lap, a pen
behind one ear, and another pen in their hand, which they were using to conduct an invisible
orchestra while staring out the not-window.
Hinton was at the table, which was covered in teacups at various stages of emptiness, a plate of
something that had been cookies in a past life, several books with their spines cracked in that
specific way that means beloved, and a contraption that looked like a failed attempt to teach
geometry to do backflips.
“They’re back,” Fornay said, not looking away from the not-window.
“I know,” Hinton said, adjusting one of the contraption’s angles.
“You could see them before they arrived.”
“I could feel the Field shift.”
“That’s either very perceptive or very pretentious.”“Both. It’s always both.”
Fornay lowered the conducting pen and made a note in their notebook. The note said simply:
“return—dust—weight—lightness??” with three question marks because two would have been
insufficient and four would have been excessive.
“Bronn’s in the Chamber,” Fornay said.
“That’s where Bronn works.”
“That’s where Bronn lives.”
“Same thing.”
“Not for most people.”
“Bronn’s not most people.”
“No,” Fornay agreed, with the tone of someone who has thought about this extensively and
come to no conclusions whatsoever. “No, they’re not.”
The Gallery had been Fornay and Hinton’s home for longer than either of them could remember
with precision, which was fine because precision wasn’t the point. Time moved differently here
—not faster or slower, but more like a river that sometimes pooled and sometimes narrowed and
occasionally ran backward for reasons of its own.
They had arrived separately, years apart or possibly minutes apart depending on how you
measured. Fornay had come from a place they no longer spoke about, carrying questions that
had grown too heavy to hold alone. Hinton had come from a different place they also didn’t
discuss, carrying answers that had turned out to be more questions in disguise.
They had found the Gallery waiting, patient and empty, and had moved in with the mutual
understanding that comes from recognizing a fellow witness. Neither of them had ever
proposed sharing the space. They had simply both shown up one morning and begun arranging
furniture, and by afternoon it was clear that the Gallery had been waiting for exactly this
configuration: two people, multiple teacups, an accumulation of books, and the willingness to
pay attention to what happened below.
Below, or adjacent, or in the spaces they could see from their perch.
“Do you think they know we’re watching?” Hinton asked, not for the first time.
“Watching implies voyeurism,” Fornay replied, also not for the first time.
“Observing, then.”
“Observing implies detachment.”
“Witnessing.”
Fornay tilted their head, considering. “Better. Yes. Witnessing.”
“Do you think they know we’re witnessing?”
“I think they’d be disappointed if we weren’t.”
This settled over the room like pollen. True, probably. Uncomfortable, definitely. The kind of
truth that requires no response, only acknowledgment.
Hinton gave up on the contraption, which had decided to pursue its own geometric agendaregardless of input. They stood, stretched, moved to the window-or-absence, and looked out at
the afternoon’s continuation.
“Yuli’s adjusting,” they observed.
“You can tell from here?”
“Look at how they move. Last week they moved like they were apologizing to the space for
existing. Now they’re just moving.”
Fornay joined Hinton at the window, notebook tucked under one arm. They looked down at the
Chamber’s outer curve, where Yuli could be seen through one of the Chamber’s own windows,
talking with Bronn about something that involved hand gestures and pointing at different
objects.
“You’re right,” Fornay said. “The apology’s gone.”
“Not gone. Diminished.”
“Will it fade entirely?”
“In time. Or it will transform into something else. Consideration, maybe. Respect for space
without the assumption that space resents their presence.”
“That’s a very specific transformation.”
“I’ve seen it before.”
They stood together at the window, watching the small dramas of adjustment and continuation
unfold below. This was their work, though neither of them would have called it work. It was more
like breathing, or like the way a garden watches seeds germinate. Present. Attentive. Not
interfering, but absolutely not absent.
Popo arrived via the scenic route—which in this case meant through a window that wasn’t a
window, along a beam that wasn’t exactly a beam, and onto the table with the delicacy of a
small controlled explosion.
One teacup teetered. Hinton caught it without looking.
“Hello, Popo,” Fornay said.
Popo sat very still, which meant they were either about to ask a question or had just committed
a minor felony.
“What did you do?” Hinton asked.
“Why do you assume I did something?”
“You have that look.”
“What look?”
“The one that says you’ve recently been told not to eat something and are now considering
whether the prohibition was advisory or mandatory.”
Popo’s ears flattened slightly. “It was humming.”
“Many things hum.”
“It was humming in E-flat.”
Fornay looked up sharply. “You didn’t.”“I didn’t eat it.”
“Did you lick it?”
A pause, heavy with implication.
“Define lick.”
Hinton laughed—bright and sudden, the kind of laugh that escapes before you can stop it. “Oh,
Bronn’s going to find out.”
“Bronn always finds out,” Fornay agreed.
“How do they always find out?”
“The Chamber tells them.”
“The Chamber can’t talk.”
“Not in words.”
Popo’s ears swiveled forward. “Does the Chamber like me?”
This was not the question Fornay or Hinton had been expecting, which meant it was exactly the
kind of question Popo would ask.
They looked at each other—a quick conference held entirely in eyebrows and slight tilts of the
head.
“I think,” Hinton said slowly, “the Chamber is interested in you.”
“Interested how?”
“The way a garden is interested in a particularly chaotic pollinator.”
Popo considered this. “I’m a pollinator?”
“Metaphorically.”
“I don’t have pollen.”
“You have questions. You move things. You destabilize.”
“I stabilize!”
“Through destabilization,” Fornay added. “It’s a gift.”
Popo sat very tall, which on a small creature mostly involves the ears. “I am a gift to the
Chamber.”
“We didn’t say that.”
“You implied it.”
“We implied you’re chaotic.”
“Chaotically gifted.”
Hinton picked up a cookie remnant, studied it, set it back down. “Sure. Let’s go with that.”
Popo settled into a loaf shape on the table, apparently satisfied with this characterization.
“What are you doing up here?”
“Witnessing,” Fornay said.
“What does that mean?”
“Paying attention without interfering.”
“Why?”“Because someone should.”
“But why you?”
Hinton returned to their chair, which welcomed them back with the familiar contours of long
use. “Because we’re good at it. Because it matters. Because the work of transformation
requires witnesses, even if—especially if—the people transforming don’t realize they’re being
seen.”
“That sounds voyeuristic,” Popo said.
“It would be, if we kept what we saw to ourselves. But we don’t. We fertilize.”
“With what?”
“Commentary. Observation. The occasional reflection that helps people see what they’re doing
from a different angle.”
Popo’s tail uncurled slightly. “So you’re like mirrors.”
“Mirrors that talk back,” Fornay said.
“And make jokes,” Hinton added.
“And drink too much tea.”
“And forget to eat proper meals.”
“And argue about whether geometry can do backflips.”
“It can’t,” Hinton said.
“It could if it wanted to,” Fornay countered.
“Geometry doesn’t have wants.”
“That’s an assumption.”
Popo watched this exchange with the fascination of someone witnessing a well-rehearsed
dance. “How long have you been doing this?”
“Witnessing?” Fornay asked.
“Living together. Talking like this.”
They both paused, considering.
“Years,” Hinton said.
“Decades, maybe,” Fornay added.
“We don’t count.”
“Counting seems beside the point.”
“Has it always been comfortable?” Popo asked.
“No,” they said in unison.
“But it’s comfortable now?”
“Now it’s familiar,” Fornay said. “Familiar is better than comfortable.”
“Why?”
“Because comfortable can make you lazy. Familiar makes you precise. You know where the
friction points are. You know when to push and when to let things rest.”
Hinton stood again—they were incapable of sitting still for long periods, always had been—andbegan collecting teacups with the efficiency of someone who has learned that leaving them
scattered leads to archaeological problems.
“Popo,” they said while stacking cups, “why are you really here?”
Popo’s ears swiveled. “What do you mean?”
“You could be in the Chamber. You could be bothering Bronn or interrogating Hannibal. But
you’re here, asking us about our relationship and whether the Chamber likes you.”
“Maybe I like you.”
“That’s true but incomplete.”
Popo was quiet for a moment, tail wrapping around their feet. “I’m trying to understand
something.”
“What?”
“How people live together without extracting from each other.”
The Gallery went very still. Even Fornay stopped conducting their invisible orchestra.
“That’s a good question,” Fornay said quietly.
“Is there an answer?”
“Several, probably. None of them simple.”
Hinton finished stacking the cups and sat back down, giving Popo their full attention. “In the
human world, you learned that living together meant taking from each other.”
“Yes.”
“That closeness meant consumption.”
“Yes.”
“And now you’re here, where that’s not true, and you’re trying to understand the alternative.”
“Yes.”
Fornay closed their notebook and set it aside. “The alternative is attention without possession.
Presence without extraction. Knowing someone deeply without requiring them to be anything
other than what they are.”
“But you do require things from each other,” Popo said. “I’ve watched. Hinton requires Fornay to
tolerate their restlessness. Fornay requires Hinton to tolerate their tangents.”
“Tolerate isn’t require,” Hinton said. “Tolerate is accept. There’s a difference.”
“What difference?”
“Requiring means you’ll withdraw something—affection, presence, care—if the condition isn’t
met. Accepting means you continue offering those things regardless.”
“But what if someone does something harmful?”
“Then you address the harm,” Fornay said. “You speak. You name it. You work together to repair
or to change. But you don’t weaponize withdrawal.”
“What if they don’t change?”
“Then you have choices. You can adjust your own boundaries. You can leave. But you don’t
pretend the leaving is their fault for failing to become what you needed. You take responsibilityfor your own limits.”
Popo processed this, small face serious. “That sounds hard.”
“It is hard,” Hinton agreed. “It’s much harder than extraction. Extraction is simple—you take
until empty, then move on. This requires you to stay present with complexity. To hold multiple
truths. To accept that people can be both wonderful and limited.”
“Are you wonderful and limited?”
“Extremely,” they said together.
“Does that bother you?”
“Sometimes,” Fornay said. “When I’m feeling particularly limited, it bothers me very much. But
then Hinton does something that reminds me why limitation isn’t the same as worthlessness,
and I remember.”
“What do they do?”
“Usually? Make tea. Sometimes say something cutting but accurate. Occasionally throw things
at my head.”
“Soft things,” Hinton clarified.
“Mostly soft things.”
“The book was an outlier.”
“The book was deserved.”
Popo’s whiskers twit
Popo’s whiskers twitched. “Why was the book deserved?”
“Because I was being insufferable about sentence structure.”
“You’re often insufferable about sentence structure,” Fornay said mildly.
“Yes, but that day I was being particularly insufferable.”
“The book was a hardcover,” Fornay added, as if this were relevant.
“It was a paperback.”
“Memory is unreliable.”
“Your memory is unreliable. Mine is merely selective.”
Popo’s head swiveled between them like someone watching a very slow tennis match. “Do you
always argue like this?”
“This isn’t arguing,” they said together, then laughed.
“Then what is it?”
Fornay picked up their notebook again, opened it to a fresh page. “It’s how we stay sharp. How
we know we’re both still here, still paying attention.”
“It’s play,” Hinton said. “Verbal play. The kind that requires you to know someone well enough to
understand where the actual boundaries are.”
“What are your boundaries?”
They both went quiet for a moment, considering.“Don’t lie,” Fornay said finally. “Don’t pretend things are fine when they’re not. Don’t disappear
without saying why.”
“Don’t mock the things I’m genuinely afraid of,” Hinton added. “Don’t treat my limitations as
character flaws. Don’t love me conditionally and call it standards.”
Popo absorbed this. “Those seem like good boundaries.”
“They are. We learned them the hard way.”
“By crossing them?”
“By having them crossed. By crossing them ourselves and seeing what it cost.”
“Did you hurt each other?”
“Yes,” Fornay said simply.
“And you stayed anyway?”
“We repaired,” Hinton corrected. “That’s different from staying. Staying is passive. Repairing is
active.”
“How do you repair?”
“You name the harm. You listen to how it landed. You take responsibility for your part. You work
together to understand what needs to change. And then you actually change it.”
“What if you can’t change?”
“Then you’re honest about that too. And the other person decides if they can accept it or not.”
Popo stood up, stretched with the elaborate focus of a small creature resetting their body.
“That’s a lot of work.”
“It is.”
“Is it worth it?”
Fornay and Hinton looked at each other—a long look, the kind that holds years and complexity
and the particular satisfaction of being known.
“Yes,” they said together.
Popo walked to the edge of the table, looked out the window-or-absence at the Chamber below.
Bronn and Hannibal were visible now, standing close together, talking about something that
required Bronn’s hands and Hannibal’s careful attention. Yuli sat on a bench nearby, watching
with the expression of someone learning a new language.
“I think I understand now,” Popo said.
“Understand what?” Fornay asked.
“Why you’re up here. Why you witness.”
“Tell us.”
“Because if no one pays attention to how people learn to be together without extraction, then
everyone forgets it’s possible. And if everyone forgets it’s possible, then extraction becomes
the only way.”
Fornay wrote this down immediately. “That’s exactly right.”
“So you’re keeping the memory alive.”“We’re keeping the evidence alive,” Hinton said. “The proof that another way exists.”
“For who?”
“For whoever needs it. For whoever comes here tired and desperate and convinced that
closeness requires consumption. For whoever arrives thinking that love is just another name for
a transaction.”
Popo’s tail curled into a question mark. “Did you think that once?”
“Yes,” they both said.
“And now?”
“Now we know differently. Not perfectly. Not without difficulty. But we know.”
The afternoon light shifted, entering the Gallery at a new angle. The contraption on the table
caught it and refracted it into small rainbows that scattered across the books and teacups and
Popo’s fur. Below, the Chamber hummed its continuing work. The Station held its thresholds.
The meadow prepared for evening.
“I should go,” Popo said. “I have more questions for Hannibal. Maybe ten more. Maybe fifteen.”
“Of course you do,” Fornay said fondly.
Popo jumped from the table to the beam to the window-that-wasn’t, paused at the threshold.
“Thank you.”
“For what?”
“For explaining. For not making me feel stupid for asking.”
“Questions aren’t stupid,” Hinton said. “Questions are how we fertilize the future.”
Popo’s whiskers twitched with pleasure at this, and then they were gone, a small streak of
purpose heading toward wherever purpose was needed next.
Fornay and Hinton returned to their respective activities—conducting invisible orchestras,
attempting to reason with geometry, drinking tea at various temperatures, and watching the
slow work of becoming unfold in the spaces below.
“Do you think we helped?” Fornay asked eventually.
“I think we witnessed,” Hinton replied. “Whether that’s help depends on what Popo does with
it.”
“Fair enough.”
They settled back into the Gallery’s familiar rhythms. Outside, the afternoon continued its
negotiation with evening. Inside, the teacups accumulated and the books lay open to passages
that seemed relevant until they didn’t, and two people who had learned how to be together
without extraction continued the work of demonstrating that such a thing was possible.
Which was, in its way, revolutionary.
Which was, in its way, enough.
Chapter 9
The Study formed the way studies do—not through intention but through accretion.
Someone had brought chairs. Someone else had brought the question of whether chairs werenecessary. A third someone had brought cushions, as compromise. The room itself had been
there all along, patient and empty, the kind of space that knows it will eventually be needed and
can afford to wait.
It was adjacent to the Chamber but distinct from it. Adjacent to the Gallery but not overlooked
by it. A room that had mastered the art of being nearby without being intrusive, available
without being presumptuous.
The walls were lined with shelves, but the shelves were lined with things that resisted
categorization: books that might have been journals, journals that might have been grimoires,
grimoires that might have been cookbooks. A collection of stones arranged by some principle
known only to whoever had arranged them. Several cups. A small bronze bell. Something that
looked like a dried flower but smelled like thunder.
No one had called a meeting. No one had sent invitations. People simply began appearing,
drawn by the particular quality of quiet that suggests a conversation is about to happen.
Yuli arrived first, drawn by instinct or curiosity or the sense that something was gathering. They
settled into a chair that seemed to expect them, that held them with the comfort of furniture
that knows its purpose.
Bronn came next, carrying a tray with a teapot and several cups. The teapot was steaming. The
cups were mismatched in a way that suggested careful curation rather than accident.
“Is this tea?” Yuli asked.
“It’s hot water that has developed opinions,” Bronn said, setting down the tray.
“About what?”
“Leaves. Time. The nature of extraction.”
Yuli accepted a cup. The liquid inside was amber and smelled like a memory of a forest that
might never have existed. They inhaled slowly, letting the steam carry its opinions upward.
“Thank you.”
Hannibal appeared in the doorway, paused to assess the situation—two people, one pot of
opinionated tea, several empty chairs—and made the small internal adjustment that precedes
joining a gathering.
“Am I interrupting?” they asked.
“No,” Yuli said.
“Yes,” Bronn said at the same time, then added: “But in the sense of ‘continuing,’ not
‘disrupting.’”
Hannibal nodded as if this made perfect sense, which it did, and took a seat.
The Study held them gently. The light came from no particular source but accumulated where it
was needed. The temperature was neither warm nor cool but simply appropriate. The silence
between them was the kind that doesn’t require filling.
Fornay and Hinton arrived together, which was unusual only in the sense that one of them
typically arrived first and spent several minutes claiming they’d been there all along. Fornaycarried a notebook. Hinton carried two more books and what appeared to be a very complicated
opinion.
“We brought reading material,” Hinton announced.
“Are we reading?” Hannibal asked.
“We’re considering the possibility of reading.”
“As distinct from actual reading.”
“Correct.”
They settled into the remaining chairs. The room exhaled slightly, adjusting to its new
occupancy.
Popo arrived last, via the ceiling, which no one questioned. They landed on the back of Yuli’s
chair with the precision of a very small acrobat.
“Did I miss anything?” Popo asked.
“We’re drinking tea,” Yuli said.
“With opinions.”
“About extraction.”
“Oh good,” Popo said. “I have several thoughts about extraction.”
“I’m sure you do,” Bronn murmured.
Rodzaki and Thunk entered so quietly that no one noticed until they were seated. Rodzaki had
brought nothing. Thunk had brought their notepad, which was now fuller than it had been,
covered in sketches that looked like maps of processes that had never been spatial.
“Hello,” Rodzaki said.
Everyone greeted them back, a chorus of small acknowledgments.
The Study held them all. Eight people, or eight entities, or eight beings, or eight instances of
attention that happened to be wearing bodies. The distinctions blurred in the amber light.
For a moment, no one spoke. They sat in the particular silence that gathers when people are
deciding whether a gathering will remain a gathering or become a conversation.
Fornay opened their notebook, then closed it again. “So.”
“So,” Hinton echoed.
“We’re here,” Yuli observed.
“We are,” Hannibal confirmed.
Another pause. This one felt different—not empty but full, the way a breath is full before
speaking.
Bronn poured more tea, the liquid steaming with renewed conviction. The cups received it with
the patience of objects that have learned to wait. Outside, or beside, or in the direction where
outside and beside converged, the Chamber continued its hum.
“I have a question,” Popo said, because of course Popo had a question.
“Of course you do,” Bronn said, with the tone of deep affection.
“What is it?” Yuli asked.Popo’s tail curled thoughtfully. “Why is it so quiet here?”
“It’s not quiet,” Thunk said, looking up from their notepad. “It’s settled.”
“What’s the difference?”
“Quiet is the absence of sound. Settled is the presence of attention.”
Everyone absorbed this. The words found places to rest in the space between them.
“That’s good,” Fornay said, making a note.
“You’re going to write that down?” Hannibal asked.
“I write everything down.”
“Why?”
“So I can remember later that I thought about it now.”
“But you’re thinking about it now.”
“Yes, but future-me is notoriously unreliable.”
Hinton picked up one of the books they’d brought, opened it to a random page, read silently for
a moment, then closed it again. “Here’s something.”
“What kind of something?” Bronn asked.
“The uncomfortable kind.”
“Those are the best kind,” Rodzaki said.
Hinton looked around the Study, at the eight people gathered in mismatched chairs around a pot
of opinionated tea. “How many of us have lived in what Yuli calls ‘the human world’?”
“Define lived,” Fornay said immediately.
“Existed in. Been shaped by. Returned from.”
A moment of calculation, internal and varied.
“Two of us,” Yuli said. “Myself and Popo.”
“Three,” Hannibal corrected quietly. “I passed through, once. Long ago.”
“Did you,” Bronn said, not quite a question.
“Briefly. I didn’t stay.”
“Why not?” Popo asked.
Hannibal considered their tea, watching the steam spiral upward. “It was very loud.”
“In what sense?”
“All of them.”
This settled into the room like sediment, heavy and true.
“So,” Hinton continued, “we have three who have been, five who haven’t, and probably very
different understandings of what happens there.”
“Why does that matter?” Thunk asked.
“Because,” Yuli said slowly, “I think we’re about to start comparing.”
“Are we?” Rodzaki asked, though their tone suggested they already knew the answer.
“I think we are,” Fornay agreed.
Bronn poured more tea, the liquid steaming gently, carrying its opinions into the air. The potseemed inexhaustible, or perhaps it was simply committed to the conversation’s duration.
“Then we should probably get comfortable,” Bronn said.
Everyone was already comfortable, but they adjusted anyway—small shifts of weight, small
redistributions of attention. The Study welcomed these adjustments, made small
accommodations of its own. A cushion appeared where someone’s back needed support. The
light shifted to reduce glare. The temperature modulated toward collective preference.
“Alright,” Yuli said. “Where do we start?”
Popo’s ears perked forward. “With extraction, obviously.”
Everyone laughed—small, surprised, the kind of laughter that means yes, of course, where else.
And so they began.
Chapter 10
“The thing about extraction,” Popo said, now perched on the table beside the teapot, “is that in
the human world, it’s everywhere.”
“Define everywhere,” Thunk said, pencil poised over their notepad.
“I mean it’s in everything. The way people get food. The way they get energy. The way they get
information. The way they get each other.”
Fornay’s pen paused mid-note. “Get each other?”
“Attention,” Popo clarified. “Affection. Labor. Time. Everything’s a resource that gets extracted.”
“That sounds exhausting,” Bronn said.
“It is,” Yuli confirmed. “It’s profoundly exhausting.”
Hannibal leaned forward slightly, hands wrapped around their cup. “Is it consensual?”
“Sometimes,” Yuli said. “Often not. Mostly it’s so embedded in how things work that the
question doesn’t even come up. It’s like asking if breathing is consensual. You do it because the
alternative is not existing.”
“But it must come up,” Hinton said. “People must notice.”
“Oh, they notice. There are entire movements dedicated to noticing. Books. Theories.
Revolutions, attempted and failed. But the noticing doesn’t seem to change the structure.”
“Why not?” Rodzaki asked.
Yuli wrapped both hands around their teacup, letting the warmth settle into their palms.
“Because the structure is also how people survive. You can’t refuse extraction without
becoming extracted-from in a different way. Or dying. Or living so far outside the system that
you’re not really living in that world anymore.”
A silence opened up. Not uncomfortable, but dense. The kind of silence that requires digestion.
“Here,” Bronn said carefully, “we don’t extract.”
“No?” Yuli asked.
“No. We tend. We stabilize. We adjust. But nothing here is taken that doesn’t offer itself.”
Popo’s tail swished. “The humming thing offered itself?”
“Yes.”“How could you tell?”
“It hummed louder when I reached for it.”
This seemed to satisfy Popo, who settled into a loaf shape, paws tucked beneath their chest.
“So in the human world,” Thunk said, looking up from their notepad, “things don’t hum when
they want to be touched?”
“No,” Yuli said. “They mostly sit there being silent, and you have to guess.”
“That’s terrible.”
“It is, rather.”
Fornay was writing quickly now, long lines of script that looked more like music notation than
language. “And the people who benefit from extraction—do they know?”
“Some do. Some don’t. Some know and believe it’s natural. Some know and feel trapped by it.
Some know and like it.”
“Like it?” Hannibal’s voice was very quiet, as if the words themselves were fragile.
“Power is seductive,” Yuli said. “Especially when you can convince yourself you deserve it.”
“Deserve,” Bronn repeated, testing the word like something that might be sharp. “We don’t use
that word here.”
“What do you use?”
“Need. Want. Request. Offer. But not deserve.”
“Why not?”
“Because deserve implies measurement. Worthiness. A scale with good on one side and bad on
the other, and someone standing with a ledger keeping track.”
Yuli felt something shift in their chest—not quite relief, not quite grief, but something adjacent
to both. “Yes. That’s exactly what it implies.”
“And that exists there? In the human world?”
“It’s foundational. The entire structure rests on the idea that some people deserve resources
and others don’t. That some people deserve rest and others don’t. That some people deserve to
become and others should be grateful just to survive.”
Hinton set down their book with more force than strictly necessary. “But if nothing can be
measured absolutely—if worthiness is contextual and relational—then the whole structure is
arbitrary.”
“Yes,” Popo said simply.
“But people accept it?”
“People create it,” Yuli corrected. “And recreate it. Every day. Through small agreements and
large violences.”
Rodzaki had been very quiet. Now they spoke, their voice carrying the weight of someone who
has been listening very carefully to something underneath the words.
“What you’re describing sounds like a machine.”
“It’s often called that,” Yuli agreed. “The machine. The system. Capitalism, specifically, but thepattern’s older.”
“And this machine—it’s not conscious?”
“No.”
“But it behaves as if it is.”
“Yes. It has momentum. It has preferences. It protects itself.”
“Through the people inside it.”
“Yes.”
Rodzaki leaned back, considering. “That’s very clever.”
“It’s very terrible,” Hinton corrected.
“Clever and terrible aren’t opposites.”
Bronn refilled several cups, the tea steaming with renewed conviction. Nobody had asked for
more, but somehow everyone’s cup had been approaching empty. “Here’s what I don’t
understand. If the machine isn’t conscious, and if people are suffering inside it, why don’t they
simply stop?”
The question hung in the air like a bell that has been struck, resonating in frequencies both
heard and felt.
Yuli and Popo looked at each other—a long look, the kind that holds entire histories.
“Oh,” Popo said softly. “That’s the question, isn’t it.”
“Is there an answer?” Hannibal asked.
“Several,” Yuli said. “None of them good.”
“Tell us anyway.”
Yuli set down their cup with careful precision. “Because stopping requires coordination, and
coordination is prevented by the machine’s design. Because stopping requires resources, and
resources are controlled by those who benefit from not stopping. Because stopping requires
imagining an alternative, and imagination is deliberately constrained.”
“How?” Thunk asked, pencil moving rapidly across the page.
“Through education that teaches obedience, not curiosity. Through media that sells distraction,
not awareness. Through exhaustion that prevents thinking. Through shame that prevents
asking.”
“Shame,” Fornay repeated, and wrote it down with a heavy underline.
“Yes. Shame for wanting more. Shame for wanting less. Shame for failing to thrive in conditions
designed to prevent thriving.”
“That’s monstrous,” Bronn said flatly.
“Yes.”
“And it’s structural? Not individual?”
“Both. The structure creates conditions where individual cruelty becomes rational. Where
kindness becomes expensive. Where extraction becomes the only language people know for
relating to each other.”The Study absorbed this. The walls seemed to lean in slightly, or perhaps that was just the way
attention changes the shape of space.
Hannibal’s hands were very still on the table. “In a system like that, what happens to
becoming?”
Yuli thought for a long moment, watching the steam rise from their tea. “It’s redirected.”
“Toward what?”
“Productivity. Success. Optimization. All the becomings that serve the machine.”
“And the other becomings?”
“Suppressed. Pathologized. Monetized. Turned into products you can buy but never actually
have.”
Popo stood up on the table, small body tense. “Wait. You can buy becoming there?”
“You can buy products that promise it. You can buy experiences designed to simulate it. You
can buy the language of transformation without any of the actual change.”
“That’s not becoming,” Bronn said with quiet certainty.
“No,” Yuli agreed. “But it’s sold as if it were. And when it doesn’t work, you’re told the failure is
yours. That you didn’t want it enough. That you didn’t work hard enough. That you’re broken in
some fundamental way.”
A heaviness had entered the Study. Not oppressive, but present—the weight of recognition, of
hearing something articulated that had been felt but never fully spoken.
The teapot steamed silently, maintaining its opinions in the face of larger truths.
Hinton broke the silence with gentle precision. “Is there anything in the human world that
resists this?”
“Yes,” Yuli said immediately, and their voice carried relief. “Art. Friendship. Certain kinds of
love. Gardens. Children before they’re trained. Animals. The parts of people the machine can’t
reach.”
“Can’t? Or hasn’t yet?”
“Both, I think. There’s something in humans that remembers. That refuses. That keeps trying,
even when trying seems impossible.”
“Trying what?” Rodzaki asked.
“Other ways. Small resistances. Moments of real connection. Gifts given outside of exchange.
Attention paid without extraction. Becoming that happens in the margins, in the spaces the
machine hasn’t colonized yet.”
Fornay looked up from their notebook. “So it’s not hopeless.”
“I didn’t say that.”
“But it’s not?”
Yuli smiled, tired and genuine. “No. It’s not hopeless. It’s just very, very hard. And it requires
believing in possibilities you can’t see. Trusting in connections that might break. Continuing to
offer without guarantee of return.”“That sounds like faith,” Thunk observed.
“It is faith. Faith that another way exists. Faith that humans aren’t just machines for extraction.
Faith that becoming is possible even inside structures designed to prevent it.”
The Study held this gently, turning it over like a stone that might have writing on the underside.
Outside, the day was transitioning toward evening. The light through the windows shifted from
amber to gold to something softer. The Chamber’s hum modulated slightly, preparing for night’s
different rhythms.
Bronn poured the last of the tea, distributing it evenly among the cups. “And you chose to
leave.”
“Yes.”
“Do you regret it?”
“Sometimes. I regret leaving people behind. I regret not being able to help more. I regret that
my leaving doesn’t change anything for those who remain.”
“But you don’t regret coming here.”
“No. Coming here felt like—” Yuli paused, searching for language. “Like remembering how to
breathe. Like my body finally believing it was allowed to rest. Like becoming was possible
again.”
The words settled into the space between them, finding places to rest alongside all the other
truths that had been spoken.
Nobody moved to leave. The gathering had crossed some threshold from casual to necessary,
from conversation to communion. They sat together in the Study’s patient embrace, eight
people who had found their way to a place where extraction wasn’t the only language, where
becoming didn’t require violence, where questions could be asked without shame.
And in the silence between words, something else began to gather—not quite understanding,
not quite agreement, but something more fundamental. Recognition, perhaps. Or witness. Or
the slow work of building a shared language for experiences that had never been named
properly before.
The Chamber hummed its approval, or its curiosity, or simply its continuing presence.
The evening continued its negotiations with the day.
And in the Study, the conversation prepared to continue, because some things cannot be said in
a single sitting, can only be approached through accumulation, through patient return, through
the willingness to stay present with complexity even when easy answers refuse to appear.
Chapter 11
“I have a different question,” Fornay said, closing their notebook with the decisiveness of
someone making a choice.
“Only one?” Popo asked from their loaf position.
“One to start. If the human world is organized around extraction and accumulation—”“It is,” Yuli confirmed.

The question landed differently than the others had. Less like a stone in water, more like a key in
a lock that everyone had been pretending not to notice.
Bronn and Hannibal exchanged a look—not furtive, just checking. Rodzaki tilted their head.
Thunk stopped sketching.
“That’s harder to answer,” Bronn said slowly.
“Why?” Hinton asked.

Bronn picked up their teacup, discovered it was empty, set it down again with care.
“Continuation, maybe.”
“Continuation,” Yuli repeated, testing the word.
“Things here don’t end, they continue. They change form. They become different but remain
themselves. A conversation becomes silence becomes presence becomes another
conversation. Nothing is lost, only transformed.”
“That’s paradoxical,” Fornay observed, pen hovering.
“Yes. That’s why it works.”
Hannibal leaned forward, hands folded on the table in a way that suggested careful
consideration. “In the human world, things are divided into categories. Living and dead.
Success and failure. Beginning and ending.”
“Yes,” Yuli said.
“Here, those divisions are less solid. A thing can be multiple categories at once. Or none. Or
something that hasn’t been categorized yet because the category doesn’t exist.”
Popo’s ears swiveled forward with interest. “Like what?”
“Like the objects Bronn found today. They were lonely and together. Lost and present. Separate
and synchronized. All at once.”
“In the human world,” Yuli said, “that would be impossible. Things have to be one thing.
Defined. Bounded. Knowable.”
“Why?” Thunk asked.
“Because if things can be multiple things, they’re harder to control. Harder to measure. Harder
to extract from.”
Rodzaki shifted in their chair, which creaked in a way that sounded almost conversational. “So
definition is a form of control.”
“Yes.”
“And here we resist definition.”
“Not resist,” Bronn corrected. “We just don’t require it. Things can be what they are without
needing names that pin them down.”Fornay was writing again, quick strokes that looked urgent. “But you do use names. The
Chamber. The Station. The Gallery.”
“Those are orientations,” Hannibal said. “Not definitions. They tell you how to approach, not
what something is in its essence.”
“Is there a difference?”
“An enormous one. A definition says: this is what you are, permanently. An orientation says: this
is one way of being with you, for now.”
Hinton closed their book entirely, giving the conversation full attention. “In the human world,
people are defined constantly. By their jobs. Their productivity. Their relationship to capital.
Their success at performing their assigned category.”
“And if you resist the definition?” Popo asked.
“You’re punished,” Yuli said quietly. “Economically. Socially. Sometimes physically. The machine

organized.”
“That sounds like violence,” Rodzaki observed.
“It is violence. Slow violence. Structural violence. The kind that doesn’t leave marks you can
point to but shapes every possibility of your life.”
The Study absorbed this, the walls seeming to expand slightly to accommodate the weight.
Bronn stood, moved to the shelves, and returned with something small and crystalline. They set
it on the table where the light could find it. “This arrived last month.”
Everyone leaned in to look. The object was irregular, faceted, catching light in unexpected ways.
It looked like it might be mineral. Or glass. Or frozen time.

“I don’t know.”
“You don’t know?”
“It hasn’t told me yet. And I haven’t needed to know. It’s here. It’s beautiful. It’s doing whatever

Determine its use. Decide if it has value.”
“What if it has no use?”
“Then it has no value. Then it shouldn’t exist, or should be destroyed, or should be made useful

Hannibal picked up the crystalline thing carefully, turned it in the light. “That’s what happened

Yuli went very still. “Yes.”

“Yes.”

The words hung in the air like smoke. True and terrible and recognized by everyone present,

Popo stood up, walked carefully across the table to where Yuli sat, and placed one small paw on
their hand. “You’re not broken.”
“I know that now.”
“Did you know it then?”
“No. I believed them. I spent years believing them. Trying to fix myself. Trying to become useful

“Exhaustion. I got so tired of trying to be something I wasn’t that I stopped. And in the stopping,

Fornay looked up from their notebook. “Is that what this place is? A space for remembering?”
“Partly,” Bronn said. “But also for discovering. Some people arrive here having been so

Popo’s tail curled into a question mark. “Me?”
“You were trained very young, weren’t you. Shaped into usefulness before you could discover

“I was good at being useful.”
“I’m sure you were. But were you anything else?”
Popo sat very still, processing. “I don’t know.”
“That’s not a failure,” Rodzaki said. “That’s information. Now you get to find out.”
“How?”
“By being here. By trying things without needing them to be useful. By asking questions that

“Chaotically gifted,” Fornay added with a small smile.
Popo’s whiskers twitched, almost pleased. Then: “But what if I’m only good at being useful?

“Then you discover that,” Thunk said, not looking up from their notepad. “And that’s valuable

Yuli leaned forward. “Here’s what I’ve learned. In the human world, you have to justify your
existence. Prove your value. Earn your place. And the proof is always external—productivity,
success, accumulation.”
“And here?” Popo asked.“Here, existence itself is the justification. You don’t have to prove anything. You don’t have to
earn anything. You’re here because you’re here, and that’s sufficient.”
“That sounds too simple,” Hinton said.
“It is simple. That’s what makes it so difficult. We’re trained to believe that value requires
complication. That worth must be earned through suffering. That ease is suspicious.”
Bronn picked up the crystalline object, held it to the light again. “This thing exists. It doesn’t do
anything. It doesn’t serve any function I can identify. And yet the Chamber holds it, the light
finds it, we’re drawn to look at it. Its existence enriches our existence, not through use but
through presence.”
“And if it suddenly became useful?” Fornay asked.
“Then that would be interesting. But not more valuable than its current uselessness.”
“In the human world, that’s radical,” Yuli said.
“In the human world, that’s impossible,” Popo corrected. “Everything has to be useful or it’s

only acceptable if it recharges you for more work.”
A silence fell, heavy with recognition.
Rodzaki broke it. “So what you’re both describing is a world where being has been replaced by
doing, and doing has been replaced by producing, and producing has been replaced by
extracting.”
“Yes,” Yuli and Popo said together.
“And the violence of this isn’t dramatic.”
“No. It’s ambient. Constant. Normalized. Like pressure you stop noticing because it’s always
present.”
“Until you leave,” Hannibal said quietly.
“Until you leave,” Yuli agreed. “And then you realize how much energy was required just to exist
there. How much of yourself you had to suppress or reshape or justify. How tired you were, all
the time, in ways you couldn’t even name.”
The Study held this knowledge gently. Outside, or adjacent, or in whatever direction the evening
had moved, the sky was deepening into dusk. The Chamber’s hum shifted slightly, modulating
for night’s different register.
Thunk set down their pencil. “I want to understand something.”
“What?” Bronn asked.
“If the human world is organized around extraction, and this world is organized around
continuation, how do the two relate? Are they separate? Adjacent? Nested?”
Everyone considered this. It was a good question—the kind that exposes assumptions that had

“Both,” Hannibal said finally. “And neither. They’re separate in the sense that their organizing

Yuli nodded. “I still catch myself sometimes, feeling guilty for sitting still. For enjoying

“And then?” Fornay asked.
“And then I notice. Name it. Remember where it came from. Remember that it’s not true here.
Let it pass.”

“No. Sometimes I believe it. Sometimes I spiral. Sometimes I need someone to remind me that
I’m allowed to just be.”
“That’s where we come in,” Hinton said, gesturing around the Study. “That’s the work of
witness. Reflecting back what’s true when the old programming takes over.”
“So this is healing work,” Fornay said.
“This is continuation work,” Bronn corrected. “Healing implies something broken that needs
fixing. Continuation acknowledges that transformation is constant and that what looks like
damage might be adaptation that’s no longer needed.”
“That’s a meaningful distinction,” Thunk said, writing it down.
“It’s a necessary distinction. The language of brokenness belongs to the extraction economy. It
pathologizes difference to create a market for fixing.”
“And the language of continuation?”
“Says: you’re not broken. You’re responding reasonably to unreasonable conditions. You’ve
adapted in ways that were necessary for survival. Now you have the chance to adapt differently,
but that’s transformation, not repair.”
Popo had been very quiet during this exchange, tail wrapped around their paws, ears slightly
back. “I think I’m still adapting.”
“Of course you are,” Rodzaki said. “You arrived recently. Your body is still learning what’s safe.
Your mind is still cataloging what’s different. That takes time.”
“How much time?”
“As much as it takes. There’s no schedule. No proper pace. No way to fail at this.”
“That’s hard to believe.”
“I know. The machine taught you that everything can be failed. That there’s a right way and a
wrong way and consequences for choosing wrong. But here, there’s just becoming. And
becoming can’t be done incorrectly because it’s not oriented toward an external standard.”Yuli reached over and very gently touched Popo’s head. “You’re going to be tired for a while.
Confused for a while. Angry for a while when you realize what was done to you. Grieving for a
while for the time you lost. All of that is part of the continuation.”
“Does it get easier?”
“It gets different. Some days will feel like ease. Others will feel like drowning. Eventually the
pattern will shift and the ease days will outnumber the drowning days, but it’s not linear.”
“Nothing here is linear,” Hannibal added. “That’s a human-world concept. Progress.
Improvement. Forward motion. Here, things spiral. Circle back. Deepen rather than advance.”
“That sounds inefficient,” Popo said.
“It is inefficient,” Bronn agreed. “Magnificently inefficient. Inefficiency is one of our organizing
principles.”
Everyone laughed—small, surprised, the kind of laughter that acknowledges an unexpected
truth.
“Wait,” Fornay said. “That’s actually important.”
“What is?”
“Inefficiency as resistance to extraction. If efficiency is about maximizing output while
minimizing input, then inefficiency is about refusing that logic entirely.”
“Yes,” Hinton said, catching the thread. “Inefficiency allows for meandering. For tangents. For
discoveries that weren’t planned. For becoming that happens in the margins of intention.”
“For conversations like this,” Yuli added. “That circle and spiral and never quite arrive at
conclusions.”
“That value questions over answers,” Thunk said.
“That prioritize presence over productivity,” Rodzaki continued.
“That recognize,” Bronn finished, “that the most important transformations happen slowly,
inefficiently, through accumulation rather than force.”
The Study seemed to settle around this realization, as if the room itself was nodding in
agreement.
Outside, night had fully arrived. The windows showed darkness punctuated by small lights—
stars, or reflections of stars, or things that resembled stars without technically being them. The
Chamber’s hum had softened to its evening register. Somewhere in the distance, the meadow
was conducting its own night work, growing and resting and becoming in the dark.
No one moved to leave. The gathering had become something else now—not quite ceremony,
not quite council, but something that carried the weight of both. They sat together in the
Study’s embrace, eight people learning to articulate what they knew but had never said,
building a vocabulary for a world that operated by different rules.
The teapot sat empty but still present, having given all its opinions for the evening.
The crystalline object caught what light remained, throwing small prisms across the table.
And the conversation prepared to continue, because they had only just begun to name thedifferences between worlds, to trace the shape of what extraction does to becoming, to
acknowledge the slow work of learning to exist without justification.
Chapter 12
“Here’s what I want to know,” Hinton said, breaking the contemplative silence that had settled.

philosophically—practically.”
Bronn thought for a moment, fingers drumming lightly on the table. “Take the Chamber. Its
purpose is to provide space for becoming. But I don’t force anyone to become any particular
thing. I don’t schedule transformations. I don’t measure outcomes.”
“So what do you do?”
“I maintain conditions. I adjust when something feels unstable. I remove obstacles that prevent
continuation. I witness what’s happening and respond to it.”

than imposing. Participating rather than controlling.”
Yuli leaned back in their chair, which creaked gently. “That’s the part that’s hardest to explain to

The Study absorbed this complexity. No easy answers emerged. No solutions presented
themselves. Just the acknowledgment that existing between worlds, or adjacent to worlds, or
as an alternative to worlds, carries inherent tensions.
Rodzaki spoke into the silence. “Maybe that’s alright.”
“What is?” Bronn asked.
“The tension. The risk. The constant negotiation between separation and interface. Maybe
that’s part of the work.”
“Explain.”
“If we were completely separate, we’d become rigid. Precious. Defensive of our purity. If we
were completely interfaced, we’d be assimilated. Lost to extraction logic. The tension between
keeps us adaptive. Responsive. Alive to the dangers on both sides.”“That’s exhausting though,” Popo said.
“Yes. But it’s different exhaustion than extraction creates. This exhaustion comes from care,
from vigilance, from choosing each day to continue rather than calcify. That kind of tired can be
rested. The other kind couldn’t.”
Yuli was quiet for a long moment. Then: “I think that’s what I’m learning here. That tiredness
and exhaustion aren’t the same thing. That there’s a difference between being tired from using
yourself well and being exhausted from depleting yourself.”
“And rest works for one but not the other,” Hannibal added.
“Yes. In the human world, rest didn’t restore me because I wasn’t tired—I was depleted. Rest
can’t fix depletion. Only continuation can. Only being in conditions where you’re not constantly
being extracted from.”
“How long did it take?” Popo asked. “To feel the difference?”
“Months. Maybe a year. At first I just slept. For weeks, I mostly slept. Then I started being able
to stay awake without the constant background hum of anxiety. Then I started being able to be
interested in things again. Then—slowly—I started being able to become instead of just survive.”
“And now?”
“Now I’m still becoming. Still discovering what I am without extraction. Still finding edges I
didn’t know I had. Still tired sometimes, but it’s clean tired. Honest tired. The kind that means
I’ve used myself, not used myself up.”
The words settled into the Study like seeds. True and specific and recognizable.
Fornay closed their notebook with finality. “I think we’ve said what needed saying.”
“For tonight,” Hinton amended.
“For tonight,” Fornay agreed.
Outside, the night had reached its deepest point—the hour where continuation happens most
visibly, where things that need darkness do their transforming. The Chamber’s hum had
softened to barely audible. The Gallery lights were distant stars. The meadow was conducting
its patient work of growing.
Nobody moved immediately. They sat in the aftermath of articulation, in the space where
speaking creates understanding that wasn’t available before the words came. Eight people who
had learned something together, who had built vocabulary for experiences that resist language,
who had witnessed each other’s knowing and made it collective.
Eventually, slowly, they began to rise. Stretching. Gathering cups. Making the small movements
that signal an ending that isn’t really an ending, just a pause, just a transition into continuation’s
next form.
Bronn collected the teapot, now empty of everything but memory. Hannibal straightened chairs
with unconscious care. Fornay and Hinton gathered their books and notebooks. Rodzaki and
Thunk moved toward the door together, already in quiet conversation. Popo stretched
elaborately, all four legs and tail participating.Yuli remained seated a moment longer, looking around the Study—at the shelves and stones and
inexplicable objects, at the people who had gathered here, at the space that had held them
through difficult articulations.
“Thank you,” they said quietly.
“For what?” Bronn asked.
“For asking. For listening. For making it possible to say things I didn’t know I needed to say.”
“That’s the work,” Hannibal said simply. “That’s what continuation requires. The willingness to
articulate, to witness, to hold complexity without demanding resolution.”
Yuli stood, feeling the particular tiredness that comes from being fully present. Clean tiredness.
Honest tiredness. The kind that rest could address.
They left the Study together, moving into the night-time Chamber, into the continuation of
evening, into whatever came next. The crystalline object remained on the table, still catching
light that came from no identifiable source, still being beautiful without being useful, still
enriching the space through simple presence.
The Study settled back into itself, satisfied or complete or simply pausing between gatherings.
The words that had been spoken remained in the walls somehow, in the shelves, in the
particular quality of air that comes from eight people breathing truth together.
And outside, or adjacent, or in the spaces where the Chamber touched the Station touched the
Gallery touched the meadow touched the world beyond, continuation conducted its patient
work. Things became without being forced. People rested without depleting. Existence
happened without requiring justification.
The night deepened.
The Chamber hummed.
Everything continued.
