Chapter 7:Bronn woke to the low, habitual complaint of a bearing somewhere along the inner curve, a sound that meant nothing catastrophic and therefore could be ignored for exactly three breaths. They used all three, flat on their back in the nest of quilts and scavenged padding slung against the Chamber’s inner skin, watching the dull procession of indicator lights roll past like a slow constellation as the torus continued its unbothered rotation.On the fourth breath the complaint lengthened, acquired a dry edge. That moved it from “ignore” to “it will bother me later.” Bronn rolled out of the nest, bare feet meeting the scuffed, warm plating of the maintenance ring, and hooked one hand into a webbed strap while the artificial gravity remembered how serious it intended to be this cycle. A screwdriver bumped their shoulder, bounced lazily, and was caught on the second try.They padded along the curve, passing the familiar markers of their own previous irritations: the ambiguous stain in the metal where a sealant had cured badly and then perfectly, the three mismatched bolts holding down a panel nobody else would ever look under, the hand-drawn arrow pointing to a junction box accompanied by, in their own cramped handwriting, “no.”The sound resolved as they approached the eastern services bulge, where cooling and waste exchange traded jokes in slow, invisible pulses. Hannibal’s voice drifted in through the conduit above, tinny from the duct speakers.“Bronn, your friend is making a new noise.”“My friends make many noises,” Bronn said, half to the air, half to the swelling panel where the bearing sat behind. “You’ll have to be more specific.”“The third main,” Hannibal replied after a pause that meant they had turned to some status board and squinted figuratively at it. “The one you promoted from secondary without consulting the union.”Bronn pressed their forehead to the panel. The vibration came through as a fine, toothy buzz, irregular but flirting with pattern. They could feel the spent heat moving under their skin.“I demoted the union,” Bronn said. “The bearing was always doing this. It’s just trying to get reassigned to something glamorous.”Thunk appeared from the other direction, shoes squeaking faintly, hands full of nothing but potential interference. They moved carefully in the partial curve, still over-aware of where “down” currently thought it was. Rodzaki trailed behind, attention distributed across every surface with greedy interest.“Is that it?” Thunk asked, stopping a little too close to Bronn’s elbow. “The—” They made an imprecise gesture that could have been “torus,” “panel,” or “entire project of existence arranged in a ring.”“This,” Bronn said, patting the panel as if soothing a pet, “is the part of the Chamber that complains when the rest of it forgets to be dramatic. Give me the small wrench.”They did not look back. After a brief silence that felt like two people looking at one another and then at a pile of unfamiliar tools, Rodzaki said, “Which one is ‘small’ in this dialect?”“The one that looks least confident,” Bronn said. “You’ll know it by its desire to be useful in situations beyond its design.”Thunk rummaged through the magnetic strip of metal that held a holy nonsense of implements along the curve. Something clanged, stayed attached by its tether, was pulled down again. Eventually a wrench with chipped yellow paint and an apologetic angle to its handle was pressed into Bronn’s hand.“Acceptable,” Bronn said. “Now hold onto something that cannot be replaced.”Rodzaki eyed the nearest handhold as if it might disagree with that assessment, chose instead to plant their back against the inner rail, one hand on a conduit that hummed with stable, entirely disinterested power. Thunk grabbed the same rail Bronn’s foot rooted against, fingers whitening.Bronn popped the panel’s fasteners with practiced disrespect. The sheet swung out, revealing the layered mess within: pipes wrapped in insulation tape that had outlived its branding, conduits tagged in three different handwriting styles, a little cluster of sensors behaving like gossiping neighbors. The bearing in question sat half-embedded in a support brace, its casing discolored around one edge like a long-healed bruise.“Is it supposed to be that color?” Thunk asked.“It’s supposed to be this color,” Bronn said, tapping the least alarming portion. “It has evolved. You’ll find the same with people. Hold the light.”Thunk fumbled for the shaft-mounted lamp, thumbed it on too bright, then dimmed it until the interior glowed with a patient yellow. Bronn wedged the wrench onto a securing collar and leaned in, feeling the familiar resistance, the less familiar tremor.“This is your job?” Rodzaki asked, not unkindly. Their voice carried a shade of disbelief that had more to do with scale than with doubt.“This is the part of my job that believes in direct cause and effect,” Bronn said. “The rest of it is more like talking to a plant that occasionally rearranges your furniture.”They cracked the collar. The bearing gave up a thin, metallic whine and then a soft clunk as it loosened in its seat. A wash of warmer air rolled out, smelling faintly of recirculated metals and someone else’s breakfast.“Does that mean something?” Thunk asked. “The smell.”“It means you’re downwind of everything you thought vanished,” Bronn said. “Hand me the cloth. No, the less clean one. I’m not going to insult it.”Hannibal’s voice came again, marginally more amused. “I see a fluctuation in your partial differential dignity.”“That’s just me standing up too fast,” Bronn replied, wiggling the bearing free and cradling it in the cloth. “How’s your own nonsense.”“Perfectly average,” Hannibal said. “Which is all we ask of systems handling high-risk transitions between ontological registers.”Rodzaki blinked. “Is that a joke?”“In their language it’s flirting,” Bronn said. “They’re proud of their averages. Keeps the Station from feeling neglected.”“Sentinel Station is fine,” Hannibal said, with the tone of someone adjusting a tie that nobody could see. “It is present, attentive, and delighted by small variances. Rodzaki, Thunk—if you look up and ten degrees to your left, that’s one of its main observation spines.”They both looked. Through a gap where the Chamber’s inner surface opened into a transparent bulge, the Station presented itself not as a monolith but as an arrangement of spines, dishes, and articulated frames floating around a more modest core. Cables and support struts unfurled like a metal forest, flexible enough that motion could be inferred but not seen.“It’s…closer than I thought,” Thunk said. “I assumed it was more…distant.”“It finds that an unproductive posture,” Hannibal said. “How is my favorite third main bearing.”“Dry, entitled, seeking attention,” Bronn said. “We are adjusting its expectations.”They set the bearing on a low shelf that had once been a crate and now had aspirations to be a workbench. Thunk leaned over it, then leaned back as if it might take offense.“Do you replace it?” they asked. “Or negotiate.”“Both,” Bronn said. They picked up a narrow brush and a small bottle of lubricant labeled in a language that no one present had learned on purpose. “Parts are like people. They last longer if they feel they have options.”Rodzaki glanced around the Chamber as if seeing it anew: the toroidal walkway cluttered with tools and memory; the hanging plants coaxed along pipes in defiance of official airflow diagrams; the hammocked storage nets holding objects that were either spare parts or art pieces, depending on which hand grabbed them first. Along the outer curve, panels folded away from the hull showed slices of machinery in progressive intimacy, some wide open, some barely cracked.“And you live here,” Rodzaki said, more a mapping statement than a question.“Where else would I live,” Bronn replied mildly. “The Chamber gets anxious if I’m gone too long. Things start arranging themselves for efficiency. We can’t have that.”Thunk laughed, short and genuine. “Is that bad.”“It’s boring,” Bronn said. “Everything lined up by function. No cross-talk. No seat cushions made out of obsolete insulation. The Chamber is at its best when it has to wonder whether the pile of coils also wants to be a chair.”Hannibal cleared their throat in a way that suggested a microphone being politely adjusted. “Speaking of efficiency, I am preparing a demonstration sequence on Sentinel Station for our guests. Bronn, are you done domestically intimidating the hardware.”“Insofar as it accepts my authority,” Bronn said, sliding the bearing back into place with a little twist. “There. It has been heard, indulged, and reminded of its job. You still buzz at me, I downgrade you to counterweight.”They tightened the collar, wiped off the excess lubricant, then nodded once to the bearing like a shared secret had been acknowledged. Thunk shut off the lamp; the interior shadows settled back into their accustomed densities.The panel closed with a dull, satisfying sound and the fasteners seated themselves as if glad to be back in a loop they understood. The complaint that had woken Bronn dwindled, faded, became indistinguishable from the broader hum.“Partial differential dignity restored,” Hannibal announced. “Bronn, I owe you a cup of something ambiguous.”“You owe me a Station that doesn’t drop surprise diagnostics into my dreams,” Bronn said. “But I’ll take the cup. Rodzaki, Thunk—do you want to see what Hannibal gets up to when they think no one’s watching.”“I was under the impression Hannibal is literally always watching,” Rodzaki said.“Exactly,” Bronn replied. They jerked their head toward a short side passage whose mouth was partly obscured by a hanging curtain made from an old thermal blanket. “Come on. Mind your heads. The Chamber pretends to be taller than it is.”They ducked through into a slightly narrower segment where the torus pinched, walls lined with racks of neatly chaotically arranged supplies: coiled cables sorted by temperament rather than gauge, labeled canisters of gases in colors that made their purpose no clearer, a stack of transparent boxes containing objects that might have been geological samples or failed prototypes or both. A low table folded out of the wall bore mug-shaped vessels in three different materials, one cracked, all clean enough.As they passed a viewport, a sliver of space swung into view again: the Station’s structures catching a distant, stale light, webs of sensor arrays angled like a field of metal flowers. Tiny lights moved along its length in slow, concerted progress.“Do you have to go outside to get there,” Thunk asked, eyes still on the Station. “Like—suits, airlocks, the whole theater.”“Sometimes,” Bronn said. “Today you get the polite route.”They thumbed a button beside a circular hatch. It irised open with commendable lack of drama, revealing a short tube lit in institutional white. The artificial gravity leaned in a different direction beyond, as if the world had quietly decided to adjust its expectations.“Sentinel Station likes to be approached on its terms,” Hannibal said. “Which in this case means pretending it is merely adjacent.”Rodzaki hesitated at the threshold. “Are we…going to mess something up.”“Almost certainly,” Bronn said. “But that’s part of the design. Come on.”The transition from Chamber to Station felt not like stepping into a new place but like slipping sideways into a slightly different attitude. The air on the other side of the tube smelled sharper, less lived-in, with an undercurrent of filtered ozone and cleaned metal. Surfaces were smoother, corners more decisively decided. The corridor they entered ran straight instead of joining the toroidal curve, with embedded strips of light that adjusted their brightness as the four figures walked.“This way,” Hannibal said, their voice more grounded now, coming not through ducts but from small grills along the wall at roughly head height. “To the observation ring.”They walked. To Thunk, the Station felt like someone’s idea of “proper”: everything labeled with small, precise sigils; access hatches flush with the wall; no dangling tools, no improvised seating. To Rodzaki, the absence of clutter was its own kind of information, saying more about Hannibal’s habits than any explanation.“This is where you work,” Thunk said, fingers twitching at their sides as if longing to touch something and then thinking better of it. “Mostly.”“This is where my attention is anchored when it isn’t anchored elsewhere,” Hannibal said. “Bronn lives inside their project. I live in the spaces between other things’ projects.”“Romantic,” Bronn muttered.They reached the observation ring: a circular room wrapped in a continuous, floor-to-ceiling viewport, with a central console rising from the floor like the tucked-in base of a tree. The artificial gravity eased, enough that a careless step lifted slightly before settling back. Outside, the Chamber’s torus glided past in majestic understatement, its outer surface studded with ports and protrusions that made more sense from this side.Rodzaki stopped just inside the threshold, taking in the full sweep. “Oh,” they said.Thunk drifted to the glass, fingertips hovering an almost-touch away. “You can see all of it,” they said softly. “Like…all at once.”“Not all,” Hannibal said. “A curated representation of all. There are blind spots. Deliberate ones.”“They mean the parts I told them not to look at,” Bronn said. “Trade secrets. Laundry.”“If you think I cannot infer the state of your laundry from the Chamber’s thermal patterns,” Hannibal replied, “you underestimate the pettiness of my curiosity.”Bronn walked to the central console and propped a hip against it, one hand resting on its smooth edge. Displays unfolded in lazy succession around them: not windows, not quite screens, more the organized surfacing of information that had always been present. Lines traced around the image of the torus; points glowed where sensors reported their boredom.“Show them something you do,” Bronn said. “The part that doesn’t end in charts.”Hannibal considered. “Very well. Rodzaki, pick a point on the Chamber. Anywhere.”Rodzaki pointed, at random but with intent, to a segment near where they guessed Bronn’s nest might be, guided by nothing more than the faint memory of the curve. The view outside magnified, centered, dissolved into a schematic overlay of that segment’s subsystems: life support, structural integrity, communication nodes, temperature gradients.“This is that region’s immediate state,” Hannibal said. “Watch.”A tiny flicker appeared in one of the temperature lines, like a hiccup. The Station annotated it, not with an alarm but with a soft halo of attention. Another flicker followed, slower, then another, until a pattern suggested itself: someone, somewhere, boiling water in a too-efficient container.“Is that—” Thunk began.“Bronn’s improvised kettle,” Hannibal said. “They wired it directly into a service loop three months ago. I modified the loop to expect its theatrics. Now, when it cycles, I route negligible surplus heat to an adjacent panel that had been sulking at being underutilized.”“Sulking,” Bronn said. “Listen to them. They have favorites.”“I optimize for emotional continuity,” Hannibal said. “If a component believes it has been forgotten, it fails in ways that disturb my averages.”Rodzaki watched as the thin halo of attention followed the heat into the grumpy panel, which brightened infinitesimally. “So you…comfort the hardware.”“I maintain stable relationships,” Hannibal said. “The Chamber functions because Bronn listens when it complains. Sentinel functions because I notice when it goes quiet.”Thunk frowned thoughtfully. “On Earth, the systems I know only make noise when something is wrong,” they said. “Silence is ‘fine.’”“That’s because your systems are built to be ignored,” Bronn said. “These are built to be in conversation.”“Not always politely,” Hannibal added. “Observe: port ninety-three.”A section of the torus’s outer surface highlighted itself. Numbers cascaded, then stabilized. A small icon pulsed in place, stubborn.“That port refuses to adhere to projected throughput curves,” Hannibal said. “No matter how I adjust surrounding flows, it insists on a minor deviation.”“Is it dangerous,” Thunk asked.“It is annoying,” Hannibal said. “Which is worse, but only to me. It arose after Bronn installed a series of redundant buffers that technically should not matter.”Bronn folded their arms. “They matter philosophically.”“This is not a philosophical deviation,” Hannibal said. “It is measurable and pointless.”Rodzaki glanced between them, then at the highlighted port. “What does it do.”“It refuses to be optimized,” Bronn said. “Which is its job.”Hannibal sighed, or produced the digital twin of a sigh. “Their stated intent was to introduce a non-harvestable slack into a resource channel. The result is that I must spend additional attention reassuring my own monitoring subroutines that nothing is wrong.”“And in the process,” Bronn said, “you get better at distinguishing ‘inefficiency’ from ‘abuse.’ You’re welcome.”Rodzaki’s mouth ticked. “You two are like a very competent argument that never ends.”“That’s work,” Bronn said.“Speaking of,” Hannibal said. “Rodzaki, Thunk—would you like to request a minor, harmless action from Sentinel Station and see how the system adjusts.”Thunk looked suddenly suspicious. “What’s the catch.”“The catch is that your request cannot be extractive,” Hannibal said. “No ‘give me more power’ or ‘accelerate that process.’ Optional kindness only.”Rodzaki considered. “Can you…make it easier for Bronn to find their tools.”Hannibal brightened so slightly it was almost a glitch. “Delightful. Within my remit. Watch.”On the displays, the Chamber’s interior glow shifted. Small tags—subvisual locators that Bronn had installed and promptly stopped using—pinged their status. The Station nudged the Chamber’s internal lighting by fractions of a lux, brightening corners where tools most often wandered away, dimming the less trafficked ceiling panels into a deeper rest.Outside the viewport, nothing visible changed. Inside, Bronn felt the difference in their bones.“You bribed the mess,” they said. “It will only encourage it.”“It will lower your swearing frequency by twelve percent,” Hannibal said. “A net gain.”Thunk folded their arms, impressed despite themselves. “So you can do small kindnesses without…cost.”“There is always cost,” Hannibal said. “The question is to whom. In this case, the cost is to my own boredom. I will have to find new ways to entertain myself.”“You can start by not waking me up with averaged dreams,” Bronn said.“That was one time,” Hannibal said. “And you slept better afterward.”Rodzaki drifted toward the glass again, watching the quiet dance of Station and Chamber. Nothing dramatic happened. No alarms, no flashes, no sudden decisions. Just the continuous, minor adjustments of two structures that had learned, mostly, how to share.“So this is…most of it,” Rodzaki said. “Your work.”“This is the visible tip of an unambitious iceberg,” Hannibal said. “Most of it is listening and declining to react.”“I told you,” Bronn said. “Glorified tea kettle supervision.”“And yet,” Hannibal said, “you have not left.”Bronn shrugged. “Someone has to make sure you don’t get lonely.”Thunk snorted. “You’re both terrible at pretending you don’t like this.”“We never claimed not to like it,” Hannibal said. “We claimed it required constant, unglamorous attention. Which it does.”The bearing they had adjusted earlier settled into its new lubrication regimen and emitted a soft, satisfied purr over the comms. Hannibal acknowledged it with a minor process reweighting; Bronn didn’t bother to look at the panel. The Chamber and the Station, for the moment, were content enough.Chapter 8:The Peanut Gallery lived off to the side in a way that did not correspond to any map. One could draw a diagram, annotate it with strict coordinates relative to the Chamber and the Station, and still not convey the essential sidewaysness of their space. It was not secret, exactly; it was just not the center of anything on purpose.To get there from Sentinel Station, Bronn and Hannibal had, at some point, agreed on a compromise route: three corridors, one ladder, a sliding door that insisted on announcing its own existence with a melodramatic chime, and finally a narrower passage where the walls leaned in conspiratorially. At the far end, a hatch bore a single addition to its standard markings: a small, hand-drawn peanut taped beside the handle, yellowed at the corners, defiantly unlabelled.Hannibal dimmed their presence as they approached, giving the impression of stepping out of earshot without actually going anywhere. “I’ll wait here,” they said, voice filtering from a speaker that had seen better installations. “Fornay asked me not to hover.”“You’re always hovering,” Bronn said. “You just lower your volume.”“Which is all any of us can do,” Hannibal replied. “Enjoy the commentary.”Bronn thumped the hatch with the side of their fist. It swung inward on a lazy mechanism that had probably been overdue for servicing for six years and had decided to interpolate that into its identity. Warm air rolled out, carrying with it the dense, familiar smell of people who spent a lot of time sitting and thinking: old textiles, heated circuitry, something sweet left over from a snack, the ghost of solvents used on stubborn components.Inside, the Peanut Gallery’s environment was less a room than a congealed series of choices. Screens and physical boards covered most of the walls, some active, some now used as convenient flat surfaces to tape paper onto. Cables slouched from ceiling to consoles, braided or knotted in places where someone had grown tired of tripping. Chairs did not match and none of them sat square; they had been acquired one at a time, each with its own history and squeak. A long, low surface along one wall hosted mugs, cups, a dangerous number of spoons, and a device whose only clear function was to make quiet gurgling noises at inopportune moments.Fornay sat half sideways in a cracked, vinyl chair, one leg draped over the arm, a tablet balanced on their stomach. They lifted their eyes without lifting their head as Bronn stepped in, then shifted their gaze past them to Rodzaki and Thunk.“You brought fresh observational material,” Fornay said. “We were about to start drawing conclusions without new data. That never ends well.”Hinton hunched at a primary console near the center of the room, fingers moving with deceptive laziness over an interface that had long ago ceased to look like anything officially sanctioned. They did not turn, but one hand lifted in a wave that took in the air, the screens, the visitors, and Bronn’s perennially smudged shirt.“Door,” Hinton said.Bronn nudged it shut with their heel. The hatch thunked into place, cutting off the Station’s more formal hum. In here, the soundscape was softer: fans behind panels, the rustle of paper when someone shifted, the occasional tap as Fornay’s foot found the metal leg of a nearby stool.“This is Rodzaki and Thunk,” Bronn said, by way of minimal introduction. “They’ve been watching me argue with bearings. Now they get to see the part where you argue with everything else.”“We don’t argue,” Fornay said. “We curate disagreements.”Thunk’s eyes went from screen to board to ceiling and back. “Is this all…data,” they asked. “About us. About Earth.”“A generous term,” Hinton said, finally spinning their chair to face them. They had a pen tucked behind one ear and another in their hand, both chewed at the cap. “Some of it’s data. Some of it’s what we wrote down because we were afraid we’d forget if we didn’t see it on a wall.”Rodzaki took a few steps farther in. On the nearest board, a printed map of some region of Earth bristled with colored marks, strings, and notes in several handwritings. None of the annotations explained themselves conclusively. Beside it, a chalked diagram traced loops and arrows that seemed to represent flows of something—resources, attention, authority—all crossing and recrossing a central void.“Do you work here all the time,” Rodzaki asked.“I live here most of the time,” Fornay said. “Work is an emergent property.”Their corner bore witness to that claim: a narrow bunk tucked under a shelf of battered books; a small suitcase half under the bed, half oublietted; a curtain that could be drawn for the illusion of privacy and had not been moved in long enough to have settled a fine dust line at its base. A single sock, exiled from its pair, hung from a cable like a surrendered flag.Hinton’s side of the room was equally claimed but more vertical. Notes were pinned in ascending layers, fresh ones tacked on top of old, their edges curling as if trying to escape. A battered jacket hung over the back of their chair, studded with patches whose original meanings had long since eroded, now functioning mainly as texture.“We get feeds,” Hinton said. “From the Chamber, from Sentinel, from Earth. We watch, we listen, sometimes we poke. We try to make sure no one is getting clever in a way that makes things worse.”“By ‘clever’ they mean ‘efficient,’” Fornay added. “We’re allergic.”Thunk drifted closer to a low table where a collection of objects sat in a loose cluster: a broken plastic toy, a polished stone, a printed circuit board with three components missing, a small, folded paper crane. None of them were labeled. All of them had been placed there on purpose.“What’s this,” Thunk asked.“A calibration pile,” Hinton said. “Things that make sense in more than one context. If we can’t explain something we’re seeing using at least one object from that pile, we take a step back.”Bronn raised an eyebrow. “When did you formalize that.”“We didn’t,” Fornay said. “It just happened.
